questions.question: Define the Chapman-Kolmogorov equation for discrete-time Markov chains. Given a transition probability matrix P, show how to compute the 3-step transition probabilities P^(3) using this equation.
keyPoints: Chapman-Kolmogorov: P^(n+m) = P^(n) × P^(m) for any non-negative integers n, m
Matrix multiplication of transition probability matrices
Computation of P^(3) = P^2 × P or P × P^2
Interpretation: probability of transitioning from state i to j in n steps

questions.question: Consider a Markov chain modeling a simplified LRU page replacement algorithm with 2 frames and 3 pages (A, B, C). The states represent the ordered pair of pages in memory. Classify all states as transient or recurrent. Justify your answer using the definition of recurrent and transient states.
keyPoints: Recurrent: state is visited infinitely often with probability 1 when starting from any accessible state
Transient: probability of returning to state is less than 1
Communicating classes and closed sets determine recurrence

questions.question: A computer network router is modeled as a discrete-time Markov chain with states 0, 1, 2 representing the number of packets in the buffer (empty, moderate, full). The transition probability matrix is: P = [[0.5, 0.3, 0.2], [0.2, 0.5, 0.3], [0.1, 0.4, 0.5]]. (a) Determine the stationary distribution π by solving πP = π. (b) Classify each state as recurrent/transient and periodic/aperiodic. (c) Explain what the stationary distribution represents in this context.
keyPoints: Solve πP = π with Σπi = 1: yields π = [π1, π2, π3]
Stationary distribution satisfies linear equations: π1 = 0.5π1 + 0.2π2 + 0.1π3, etc.
All states communicate → same class; check period of each state (gcd of return times)
Stationary distribution gives long-run probability of buffer being in each state

questions.question: Model an M/M/1 queue (single server with Poisson arrivals and exponential service times) as a continuous-time Markov chain with state space {0, 1, 2, ...}. (a) Write the generator matrix Q for this birth-death process. (b) Define the global balance equations and derive the stationary distribution. (c) Show that for λ < μ (stability condition), the stationary distribution exists and equals πi = (1 - ρ)ρ^i where ρ = λ/μ. (d) Explain how this stationary distribution helps in analyzing system performance metrics like average queue length.
keyPoints: Generator Q: diagonal elements qii = -(λ + μ) for i≥1, q01 = λ, qii+1 = λ, qii-1 = μ
Global balance: πiλ = πi+1μ for i≥0 (flow into state equals flow out)
Solving yields geometric distribution: πi = (1-ρ)ρ^i for ρ < 1
Expected queue length L = ρ/(1-ρ); utilization = ρ; useful for system design
